docs.title: Arc
docs.text:
Arc is an AI workspace with two modes:

  • a main agent you talk to directly in chat
  • optional task agents that pick up queued background work

You can start simple with one chat session, then add multi-agent automation only when you need it.

docs.title: Fastest Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mohit17mor/Arc.git
cd Arc
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"
playwright install chromium
arc init
arc chat

When you want the dashboard, background task processing, and channels, start:

arc gateway

The dashboard runs at http://localhost:18789.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
• Getting Started: fastest path from install to first chat
  • What Arc Is: the mental model in plain language
  • Main Agent vs Task Agents: understand the two agent types
  • Capabilities Overview: browser, memory, code tools, skills, MCP, and more
  • Multi-Agent Overview: task agents, queues, chains, and reviews
  • Providers Overview: global provider setup and per-agent overrides

docs.title: What Arc Can Do
docs.text:
• Chat with a local or cloud model
  • Run a web dashboard for chat, task management, logs, and agent management
  • Queue tasks for named background agents
  • Chain multiple agents in sequence with optional reviewers
  • Use built-in tools like browser automation, code intelligence, memory, scheduler, and voice input
  • Extend the system with custom skills or external MCP servers

docs.title: Choose Based On What You Want
I just want a good local AI assistant
docs.text: Start with Getting Started, run arc init, then use arc chat.

docs.title: I want multiple specialized agents working in the background
docs.text: Go to Multi-Agent Overview.

docs.title: I want to connect more tools
docs.text: Read Skills and MCP.

docs.title: I want to tune providers and models
docs.text: Read Providers Overview and Per-Agent Provider Config.

docs.title: Micro-Kernel Design
docs.text:
┌──────────────┐
                         │  CLI / Chat  │
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
                    ┌───────────▼───────────┐
                    │      Agent Loop       │
                    │  think → act → observe│
                    └──┬─────┬─────┬─────┬──┘
                       │     │     │     │
                 ┌─────▼┐ ┌──▼──┐ ▼   ┌─▼────────┐
                 │ LLM  │ │ Mem │ │   │ Security  │
                 └──────┘ └─────┘ │   │  Engine   │
                              ┌───▼───┴───────────┘
                              │   Skill Router
                              │  (two-tier selection)
                              ├────────────────────┐
                              │ Tier 1: Always On  │
                              │ files · terminal   │
                              │ worker · task_board│
                              ├────────────────────┤
                              │ Tier 2: On Demand  │
                              │ browser · scheduler│
                              │ code_intel · MCP   │
                              └────────────────────┘

    ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
    │   CLI    │  │ WebChat  │  │ Telegram │  │  Voice   │
    │ arc chat │  │ Gateway  │  │  arc tg  │  │arc listen│
    └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘
         └──────────────┴─────────────┴──────────────┘
                       All share the same agent

Every subsystem is independent, wired through an event bus. The kernel composes:

  • EventBus — pub/sub with wildcard matching + middleware chain
  • Registry — typed service locator (DI by category + name)
  • Config — Pydantic models with 5-level precedence

docs.title: Two-Tier Skill Router
docs.text:
Sending every tool spec to the LLM on every call wastes tokens (~2,500). The router keeps it lean (~800):

Tier 1 — always sent: filesystem, terminal, worker, task_board

Tier 2 — activated on demand via use_skill("browsing"): browser, scheduler, code_intel, liquid_web, MCP, workflows

New skills auto-appear in the use_skill menu.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Location Purpose Kernel arc/core/kernel.py Central coordinator (~130 lines) EventBus arc/core/bus.py Pub/sub + middleware AgentLoop arc/agent/loop.py think → act → observe cycle SkillManager arc/skills/manager.py Skill registration + tool routing SkillRouter arc/skills/router.py Two-tier tool selection MemoryManager arc/memory/manager.py Three-tier memory orchestrator SecurityEngine arc/security/engine.py Capability checking + approval TaskProcessor arc/tasks/processor.py Background task queue processing TaskStore arc/tasks/store.py SQLite persistence for tasks BrowserEngine arc/browser/engine.py Playwright browser management LLMProvider arc/llm/base.py Abstract LLM interface GatewayServer arc/gateway/server.py WebSocket + REST API + dashboard

docs.title: LLM Providers
docs.text:
All LLM access goes through the LLMProvider interface. Implementations:

  • OllamaProvider — local models
  • OpenAICompatProvider — OpenAI, Groq, OpenRouter, Together, DeepSeek, LM Studio, etc.
  • ResponsesProvider — OpenAI Responses API
  • MockProvider — for testing

Swap providers by changing one config line. Agent code never calls LLM APIs directly.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: This page is grouped by what users usually want to do, not just by command family.

docs.title: Setup And Core Use
docs.text: Command What it does arc init First-time setup wizard for the main agent and default provider arc chat Start an interactive chat with the main agent arc gateway Run the dashboard and background systems arc listen Start voice input arc telegram Run Arc as a Telegram bot arc config Show current configuration arc version Show Arc version

docs.title: Task Agents
docs.text:
Command What it does arc agent create <name> Create a named task agent arc agent list List task agents arc agent remove <name> Delete a task agent

Common flags for arc agent create:

Flag Meaning --role, -r Role description --personality, -p Personality text --model, -m Model in provider/model format --max-concurrent Maximum parallel tasks for that agent

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
Command What it does arc task add "<title>" Queue a task arc task list List tasks arc task show <id> Show task detail and comments arc task cancel <id> Cancel a task arc task reply <id> "<text>" Approve, revise, or answer a task

Common flags for arc task add:

Flag Meaning --assign, -a Assign to a task agent --priority, -p Lower number means higher priority --max-bounces Review loop limit --after Make this task wait for another task

Common flags for arc task reply:

Flag Meaning --action, -a approve or revise

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Command What it does arc workers Show worker activity arc workers --follow Live-tail worker activity arc logs Show today's logs

docs.title: Slash Commands In Chat
docs.text: Command What it does /help Show available commands /status Show gateway status /skills List loaded skills and tools /mcp Show MCP server status /memory Show core memory /memory episodic Show episodic memory /memory forget <id> Delete a core memory fact /jobs List scheduled jobs /jobs cancel <name> Cancel a scheduled job /workflow List workflows /workflow <name> Run a workflow /cost Show token usage for the current session /clear Reset the current conversation

docs.title: Most Common First Commands
docs.text:
arc init
arc chat
arc gateway

If you are exploring multi-agent features after that:

arc agent create researcher --role "Deep web research" --model ollama/llama3.2
arc task add "Research AI coding tools" --assign researcher

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Arc keeps most persistent user configuration under ~/.arc/.

This page is the reference for where things live and how config is resolved.

docs.title: The Most Important Files
docs.text: Path Purpose ~/.arc/config.toml Main config, including the default provider ~/.arc/identity.md Main agent identity and prompt material ~/.arc/agents/*.toml Named task agent definitions ~/.arc/skills/*.py Custom Python skills ~/.arc/skills/*.md Soft skills ~/.arc/mcp.json MCP server config ~/.arc/workflows/*.yaml YAML workflows ~/.arc/tasks.db Task queue database ~/.arc/scheduler.db Scheduler database ~/.arc/logs/ Log files

docs.title: Global LLM Config
docs.text:
The default provider configured by arc init is stored in ~/.arc/config.toml.

Example:

[llm]
default_provider = "ollama"
default_model = "llama3.2"
base_url = "http://localhost:11434"
api_key = ""

worker_provider = ""
worker_model = ""
worker_base_url = ""
worker_api_key = ""

docs.title: Identity And Personality
docs.text:
The main agent identity is stored separately from named task agents.

Relevant config includes:

[identity]
path = "~/.arc/identity.md"
personality = "helpful"
user_name = ""
agent_name = "Arc"

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Common environment overrides:

Variable Maps to ARC_LLM_PROVIDER llm.default_provider ARC_LLM_MODEL llm.default_model ARC_LLM_BASE_URL llm.base_url ARC_LLM_API_KEY llm.api_key

docs.title: Config Precedence
docs.text:
Highest to lowest:

  1. explicit runtime overrides
  2. environment variables
  3. project config such as ./arc.toml if used
  4. user config in ~/.arc/config.toml
  5. built-in defaults

docs.title: Other Config Sections
docs.text:
Depending on what you enable, config.toml may also contain:

  • [memory]
  • [scheduler]
  • [telegram]
  • [tavily]
  • [ngrok]
  • [voice]
  • [gateway]

Use arc init for the common first-time setup, then edit the file directly when you need precise control.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
This page is the shortest path to a working Arc setup.

You do not need to create task agents to get started. The main agent works immediately after arc init.

docs.title: 1. Install Arc
docs.text:
Requirements:

  • Python 3.11+
  • One model provider you can reach: Ollama locally, or a cloud API
git clone https://github.com/mohit17mor/Arc.git
cd Arc
python -m venv .venv
WindowsmacOS / Linux 
.venv\Scripts\activate

source .venv/bin/activate

pip install -e ".[dev]"
playwright install chromium

docs.title: 2. Run The Setup Wizard
docs.text:
arc init

The wizard sets up your main agent identity and your default provider.

It walks through:

  1. your name and the agent name
  2. the main agent personality or custom system prompt
  3. the default LLM provider and model
  4. optional extras like Telegram, Tavily, and ngrok

Arc stores this in ~/.arc/config.toml and ~/.arc/identity.md.

docs.title: 3. Start Your First Chat
docs.text:
arc chat

This is the easiest way to understand Arc. At this point you already have:

  • the main agent
  • your default provider
  • built-in capabilities
  • any local skills and MCP config you have added

docs.title: 4. Run The Gateway When You Need More Than Chat
docs.text:
arc gateway

Use arc gateway when you want:

  • the web dashboard
  • task processing for named task agents
  • Telegram or other connected channels
  • real-time logs and background services

Open http://localhost:18789 in your browser.

docs.title: Add named task agents
docs.text: If you want background workers for specialized jobs, go to Create Agents.

docs.title: Queue tasks and chains
docs.text: If you want work to continue without staying in chat, go to Queue Tasks and Multi-Step Chains and Reviews.

docs.title: Add more tools
docs.text: If you want to extend Arc, go to Skills and MCP.

docs.title: First-Day Mental Model
docs.text:
• arc init configures the main agent and the default LLM settings.
  • arc chat talks to the main agent.
  • arc gateway turns on the dashboard and background systems.
  • Task agents are optional. You only create them when you want queued multi-agent work.

docs.title: Build Your Own Skill
docs.text: If Arc almost does what you want but needs one more local capability, a custom skill is usually the right answer.

docs.title: Soft skill
docs.text:
Use a markdown skill if you only need instruction text.

Good examples:

  • output format rules
  • team conventions
  • domain expertise reminders
  • tone and citation standards

Create:

  • ~/.arc/skills/my_rules.md

docs.title: Python skill
docs.text:
Use a Python skill if you need an actual tool call.

Good examples:

  • a wrapper around an internal script
  • a custom API call
  • a local file processing tool
  • a small automation that should feel native in Arc

Create:

  • ~/.arc/skills/my_skill.py

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
1. Start with the smallest useful tool.
  2. Keep parameters simple and explicit.
  3. Return clear success or failure text.
  4. Restart Arc and verify it appears in /skills.

docs.title: Minimal Python Example
docs.text:
from arc.skills.base import Skill
from arc.core.types import SkillManifest, ToolResult, ToolSpec

class PingSkill(Skill):
    def manifest(self) -> SkillManifest:
        return SkillManifest(
            name="ping_skill",
            version="1.0.0",
            description="Simple example skill",
            tools=(
                ToolSpec(
                    name="ping",
                    description="Return pong",
                    parameters={"type": "object", "properties": {}, "required": []},
                ),
            ),
        )

    async def execute_tool(self, tool_name, arguments):
        return ToolResult(tool_call_id="", success=True, output="pong")

docs.title: Where To Learn More
docs.text:
• Skills for how Arc discovers and loads them
  • MCP if you might prefer an external server instead of a local Arc extension

docs.title: MCP
docs.text:
MCP stands for Model Context Protocol.

In Arc, MCP is the easiest way to connect external tool servers without writing Arc-specific integration code.

docs.title: What MCP Is For
docs.text:
Use MCP when you want Arc to access tools from:

  • GitHub or other developer services
  • external file or database servers
  • internal automation servers
  • any service that already exposes an MCP server

docs.title: How Arc Uses MCP
docs.text:
You configure servers in:

  • ~/.arc/mcp.json

Arc discovers those servers and exposes them through a gateway layer.

From the model's point of view, the main entry points are:

  • mcp_list_tools
  • mcp_call

That keeps the model surface small even when you connect many MCP servers.

docs.title: Example Config
docs.text:
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "github": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
      "env": { "GITHUB_TOKEN": "ghp_xxx" }
    },
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/path/to/dir"]
    }
  }
}

Remote SSE servers are also supported.

docs.title: How To Check MCP In Arc
docs.text:
Use:

/mcp

That shows configured server status.

Use:

/skills

to see the MCP gateway alongside the rest of Arc's capabilities.

docs.title: When To Prefer MCP Over A Skill
docs.text:
Prefer MCP when:

  • the tool already exists as an MCP server
  • multiple clients may share the same tool server
  • you want a cleaner boundary between Arc and the external system
  • the integration should stay provider-agnostic

Prefer a local Arc skill when the behavior is tiny, local, or very Arc-specific.

docs.title: Capabilities Overview
docs.text:
Capabilities are the tools and systems Arc can use beyond plain chat.

This section is about what Arc can do, when you would use it, and how to extend it.

docs.title: Built-In Capabilities
docs.text:
Arc already ships with a strong base set:

  • Browser Automation: drive a real Chromium browser through structured page state
  • Web Dashboard: browser UI for chat, tasks, agents, logs, and status
  • Memory: persistent memory across sessions
  • Code Intelligence: code-aware repo mapping and symbol search
  • Voice Input: wake word plus speech-to-text flow
  • Scheduler: recurring and one-shot jobs
  • Liquid Web: product search and comparison flow

docs.title: Extension Paths
docs.text: Arc supports three main ways to add more capability.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Skills are Arc-native extensions.

Use them when you want:

  • local custom tools
  • domain instructions
  • reusable behavior that feels built into Arc

Read Skills.

docs.title: MCP
docs.text:
MCP lets Arc talk to external tool servers.

Use it when you already have an MCP-compatible server or want to connect to an external tool ecosystem without writing Arc-specific code.

Read MCP.

docs.title: Build Your Own Skill
docs.text: If you want your own local Arc extension, start with Build Your Own Skill.

docs.title: Which One Should You Use?
docs.text:
• Use a built-in capability when Arc already does what you need.
  • Use a skill when you want a local Arc-native extension.
  • Use MCP when the tool already exists as an MCP server or should stay outside Arc.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Skills are one of Arc's main extension systems.

They let you add either:

  • executable tools written in Python
  • plain-text instruction files that shape agent behavior

docs.title: What Skills Are For
docs.text:
Use skills when you want Arc to gain:

  • new local tools
  • reusable team instructions
  • domain-specific behavior
  • custom workflows without editing the core app

docs.title: Built-In Skills
docs.text:
Arc already loads built-in skills at startup.

Examples include:

  • filesystem access
  • terminal execution
  • task delegation
  • browsing
  • browser control
  • code intelligence
  • scheduler tools
  • workflow tools
  • MCP gateway tools

You can inspect loaded skills from chat with:

/skills

docs.title: 1. Python Skills
docs.text:
Put a .py file in ~/.arc/skills/.

Use this for custom tools with executable logic.

Example skeleton:

from arc.skills.base import Skill
from arc.core.types import SkillManifest, ToolResult, ToolSpec

class MySkill(Skill):
    def manifest(self) -> SkillManifest:
        return SkillManifest(
            name="my_skill",
            version="1.0.0",
            description="Does something useful",
            tools=(
                ToolSpec(
                    name="my_tool",
                    description="Run my custom action",
                    parameters={"type": "object", "properties": {}, "required": []},
                ),
            ),
        )

    async def execute_tool(self, tool_name, arguments):
        return ToolResult(tool_call_id="", success=True, output="Done")

Restart Arc and the skill is discovered automatically.

docs.title: 2. Soft Skills
docs.text:
Put a .md file in ~/.arc/skills/.

Use this for instruction-only additions such as:

  • writing rules
  • company conventions
  • domain language
  • persistent reminders about how the agent should behave

Example:

Always cite the source and date for factual claims.
Use concise bullet summaries before long explanations.
Prefer internal naming conventions used by the team.

docs.title: Where Skills Apply
docs.text:
Bundled strategy text and user soft skills are added into the prompting layer used by Arc.

In practice, skills are a good fit when you want Arc to feel smarter everywhere without building a full external integration.

docs.title: When To Use A Skill Instead Of MCP
docs.text:
Prefer a skill when:

  • the logic should live locally in Arc
  • you want a very small custom extension
  • you want direct Python control
  • you want instruction files, not just tools

If the tool already exists as an MCP server, MCP may be easier.

docs.title: Main Agent vs Task Agents
docs.text: This is the most important distinction in Arc.

docs.title: Main Agent
docs.text:
The main agent is the assistant you talk to directly.

It is used by:

  • arc chat
  • the WebChat inside the dashboard
  • connected channels like Telegram

The main agent gets its identity from the files created by arc init.

That includes:

  • your selected personality preset or custom system prompt
  • your chosen default provider and model
  • global configuration like memory, tools, and channels

docs.title: Task Agents
docs.text:
Task agents are optional named workers stored as files in ~/.arc/agents/*.toml.

Each one can have its own:

  • name
  • role
  • personality
  • full system prompt
  • provider and model override
  • base URL and API key override
  • skill allowlist or denylist

They only matter when you queue background tasks.

docs.title: When you use a preset or custom prompt in arc init
docs.text:
That affects the main agent only.

It does not automatically rewrite the prompts for named task agents.

docs.title: When you create a task agent
docs.text:
That agent uses its own configuration from the agent TOML file or the dashboard agent form.

If a task agent does not define its own LLM override, it falls back to the global default provider from ~/.arc/config.toml.

docs.title: Main agent
docs.text: You might configure the main agent to be your general-purpose assistant with a strong custom system prompt.

docs.title: Task agents
docs.text:
You might also create:

  • researcher on a cheap fast model
  • writer on a better writing model
  • reviewer on a stricter review prompt

That split is normal in Arc.

docs.title: Rule Of Thumb
docs.text:
• Change arc init when you want to change how Arc talks to you.
  • Create or edit task agents when you want to change how background workers do jobs.

docs.title: Tasks, Chains, and Reviews
docs.text: Arc supports both simple queued tasks and multi-step chains.

docs.title: Single Task
docs.text:
A single task is one unit of work assigned to one task agent.

Example:

arc task add "Research AI coding tools" --assign researcher

This is the simplest background job model.

docs.title: Chain
docs.text:
A chain is one task that moves through multiple task agents in sequence.

Example mental model:

  • step 1: researcher
  • step 2: writer
  • step 3: reviewer or human review

Arc stores the chain as one task with multiple steps.

docs.title: Dependency Between Separate Tasks
docs.text:
Arc also supports separate tasks that wait for earlier tasks.

arc task add "Research AI frameworks" --assign researcher
arc task add "Compare the top 3" --assign researcher --after t-001
arc task add "Write recommendation" --assign writer --after t-002

--after means the later task depends on the earlier task finishing first.

That is different from a multi-step chain:

  • --after links separate tasks
  • a chain keeps multiple steps inside one task

docs.title: How Handoff Works Inside A Chain
docs.text:
When one step finishes, Arc passes the earlier task activity forward as context.

The next agent sees:

  • the main task title and instruction
  • a note that earlier steps are complete
  • previous task activity and comments
  • any review feedback that needs to be addressed

The current step prompt explicitly tells the next agent to continue from previous work and not repeat earlier work unless necessary.

docs.title: Reviews
docs.text:
A step can optionally be reviewed by:

  • another task agent
  • a human

Possible outcomes:

  • approve: move to the next step
  • needs revision: send work back to the agent who just did that step

docs.title: Bounce Limits
docs.text:
Each task has max_bounces.

In a multi-step chain, the bounce counter is effectively used per review stage, not across the whole chain, because Arc resets it when moving to the next step.

docs.title: Important Current Limitation
docs.text:
A multi-step chain currently shares one overall task instruction.

The step definition itself stores:

  • which agent should handle the step
  • which reviewer, if any, should review it

It does not currently store a separate instruction per step.

So if you want strong step-by-step guidance today, put that guidance into the main task description in a clear structured way.

docs.title: What Arc Is
docs.text: Arc is an AI system that can work in two layers.

docs.title: Layer 1: Your Main Agent
docs.text:
This is the assistant you talk to directly in arc chat, WebChat, or connected channels.

Use it for:

  • normal conversation
  • ad hoc research or coding help
  • planning and execution in a single chat
  • creating tasks for background agents when needed

If all you want is a strong AI assistant with tools, this layer may be enough.

docs.title: Layer 2: Optional Task Agents
docs.text:
These are named background workers such as researcher, writer, or reviewer.

Use them when you want:

  • specialized roles
  • different models for different jobs
  • persistent queued tasks
  • multi-step chains like A -> B -> C
  • review loops without staying in chat

docs.title: Why Arc Feels Different
docs.text:
Most tools stop at one conversation.

Arc can also:

  • remember global configuration and identity
  • queue work in a persistent task board
  • move work across multiple agents
  • expose capabilities through a dashboard
  • grow with local skills and MCP servers

docs.title: The Simplest Way To Think About It
docs.text:
• The main agent is your front door.
  • Task agents are your background workers.
  • The gateway runs the dashboard and background processing.
  • Skills and MCP are how Arc gets more tools.

docs.title: When To Stay Simple
docs.text:
Use only the main agent if:

  • you mostly chat interactively
  • you do not need background queues
  • one model and one identity are enough

docs.title: When To Add Multi-Agent
docs.text:
Add task agents if:

  • you want different roles with different prompts
  • you want work to continue after you leave chat
  • you want reviewer loops or chains
  • you want different providers or models per worker

docs.title: Browser Automation
docs.text: Arc opens a real Chromium browser and interacts with pages like a human — using accessibility tree snapshots, not screenshots.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Each page is converted to a numbered list of interactive elements:

[3] textbox "Where from?" value="Delhi"
[5] combobox "Where to?"
[7] textbox "Departure" value=""
[9] button "Search"

The LLM sees this, decides what to do, and sends actions:

{"actions": [
  {"type": "fill", "target": "[5]", "value": "Mumbai"},
  {"type": "fill", "target": "[7]", "value": "2026-04-10"},
  {"type": "click", "target": "[9]"}
]}

docs.title: Browser Tools
docs.text: Tool Description browser_go Navigate to a URL, get a structured page snapshot browser_look Re-examine the current page browser_act Click, fill, scroll, submit — all in one call

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
• Fills forms (text, dropdowns, comboboxes, date pickers)
  • Handles autocomplete suggestions (Google Flights, Amazon, etc.)
  • Navigates calendars, picks dates, closes overlays
  • Deals with CAPTCHAs by asking you to solve them, then continues
  • Persistent browser profiles (cookies/sessions survive restarts)
  • Switches between headless and headed mode for human assist

docs.title: Under the Hood
docs.text:
The engine handles complex interactions mechanically:

  • Autocomplete — types char-by-char, waits for dropdown, picks best match using word-boundary-aware scoring
  • Calendars — detects calendar type (data-iso, aria-label, gridcell), navigates months, clicks the right day
  • Overlays — escalating click fallbacks (normal → force → JS → mouse coordinates)
  • CAPTCHAs — detected and escalated to human, then continues where it left off

docs.title: Why Accessibility Tree?
docs.text: Screenshot + Vision Accessibility Tree Speed Slow (image encoding + vision model) Fast (text only) Cost Expensive (vision tokens) Cheap (text tokens) Accuracy Approximate coordinates Exact element targeting Works with Vision-capable models only Any LLM

docs.title: Code Intelligence
docs.text: AST-aware code navigation using tree-sitter and grep-ast. Agents understand codebases structurally, not just as text files.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Tool Description repo_map(path) Condensed project overview — all files with classes, functions, methods find_symbol(name, path) Locate any function/class definition and get the full source body search_code(pattern, path) Grep with AST context — shows matches within their enclosing function/class scope

docs.title: Example: repo_map
docs.text:
Repository map: 9 files, 120 symbols

bus.py
  class EventBus:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
    def on(self, event_type: str, handler: EventHandler) -> None:
    async def emit(self, event: Event) -> Event:
    def emit_nowait(self, event: Event) -> None:

config.py
  class AgentConfig(BaseModel):
  class SecurityConfig(BaseModel):
  class LLMConfig(BaseModel):

One call → the agent has a mental map of the entire project. ~500-2000 tokens for a typical project.

docs.title: Example: find_symbol
docs.text:
Found 1 definition(s) of 'AgentLoop':

── loop.py (line 61) ──
  61 | class AgentLoop:
  62 |     """The agent's execution loop."""
  63 |     ...
  64 |     async def run(self, user_input: str) -> AsyncIterator[str]:

Returns the full function/class body with line numbers.

docs.title: Example: search_code
docs.text:
Found 3 match(es) across 2 file(s):

── core/bus.py (1 matches) ──
  28│class EventBus:
  ...
  98█    def emit_nowait(self, event: Event) -> None:

Shows the match highlighted (█) with its enclosing class/function scope for context.

docs.title: Supported Languages
docs.text: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, TSX, Rust, Go, Java, C, C++, C#, Ruby, PHP, Swift, Kotlin, YAML, TOML, JSON, Bash.

docs.title: Usage for Coding Tasks
docs.text:
When assigning coding tasks to agents, include the repo path in the instruction:

arc task add "Fix the auth bug in /home/user/projects/my-api" --assign developer

Or set the repo path in the agent's system prompt:

system_prompt = """
You are a backend developer working on the my-api project.
Project path: /home/user/projects/my-api

Always start with repo_map to understand the codebase before making changes.
"""

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install grep-ast  # included in Arc's default dependencies

Installed automatically with Arc. Uses tree-sitter-language-pack for multi-language grammar support.

docs.title: Web Dashboard
docs.text:
The dashboard is the browser UI served by arc gateway at http://localhost:18789.

Use it when you want a visual way to work with Arc instead of staying in the terminal.

docs.title: What The Dashboard Is Good For
docs.text:
• chatting with the main agent in the browser
  • monitoring queued and running tasks
  • creating and managing task agents
  • creating single-step or multi-step tasks
  • reviewing work and replying to blocked tasks
  • checking loaded skills, MCP status, and system logs

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: System overview: task counts, agent counts, uptime, and high-level status.

docs.title: Chat
docs.text: Browser-based chat with the main agent. This is the same main-agent layer as arc chat, just in a web UI.

docs.title: Task Board
docs.text:
Kanban-style task view for:

  • queued work
  • active work
  • review states
  • completed work

The task form supports both:

  • simple single-agent tasks
  • multi-step chains with one agent per step and an optional reviewer per step

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
Create and manage named task agents.

The form supports the full per-agent model block, including:

  • provider
  • model
  • base URL
  • API key
  • system prompt

So if a task agent needs its own endpoint, users can configure it from the dashboard.

docs.title: Scheduler
docs.text: View scheduled jobs and cancel them.

docs.title: Skills And MCP
docs.text: Inspect the currently loaded capabilities and MCP server status.

docs.title: Logs
docs.text: Watch real-time activity from the main agent, task agents, workers, and other background systems.

docs.title: Architecture Notes
docs.text:
The dashboard is a lightweight single-page app served directly by Arc.

  • no separate frontend build is required
  • one WebSocket connection powers live updates
  • REST endpoints provide overview, tasks, agents, scheduler, skills, MCP, and logs

docs.title: Liquid Web — Product Search & Comparison
docs.text:
Ask Arc to find products and it renders a live 3D carousel you can browse in your browser.

You:  "find me the best wireless earbuds under ₹5000"
Arc:  *searches → scrapes → renders → serves*
      "Found 12 products from 4 sites. Comparison page is live at: http://localhost:63350"

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Search — queries Tavily API to find relevant product pages
  2. Scrape — launches parallel headless Chromium contexts via BrowserPool
  3. Extract — pulls structured data using JSON-LD, OpenGraph, and DOM heuristics
  4. Filter — scores products on quality and drops noise
  5. Deduplicate — removes near-duplicate products by name similarity
  6. Render — generates a responsive 3D carousel UI
  7. Serve — local HTTP server (optionally tunneled via ngrok)

docs.title: Server Lifecycle
docs.text:
• Runs independently in the background
  • Auto-shuts down after 10 minutes of inactivity
  • A new search replaces the old one
  • Cleans up when you exit arc chat

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
[tavily]
api_key = "tvly-..."    # get a free key at tavily.com

[ngrok]
auth_token = "..."      # optional, for public URLs

docs.title: Three-Tier Memory
docs.text: Arc remembers you across sessions using three tiers of local SQLite storage.

docs.title: Tier 1 — Session Memory (RAM)
docs.text: Current conversation messages. Token-budget managed — older messages are truncated to fit the context window.

docs.title: Tier 2 — Episodic Memory (sqlite-vec)
docs.text: Past conversation chunks stored with vector embeddings. Retrieved by semantic similarity when relevant to the current query. Uses FastEmbed (BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5, 384-dim, fully local).

docs.title: Tier 3 — Core Memory (SQLite)
docs.text: High-confidence facts about you — name, preferences, projects. Always injected into the system prompt. Distilled automatically from conversations by an LLM background task.

docs.title: How It Flows
docs.text:
1. Context composition — before each LLM call, the ContextComposer assembles:
  2. System prompt + core facts (Tier 3, always present)
  3. Relevant episodic memories (Tier 2, retrieved by similarity to current query)

  4. Recent session messages (Tier 1, as many as fit in the token budget)

  5. After each turn — background tasks:

  6. Store the turn as episodic memory (Tier 2)
  7. Every 5 turns, distill stable facts to core memory (Tier 3)

docs.title: Chat Commands
docs.text:
/memory              # show core facts
/memory episodic     # show recent episodic memories
/memory forget <id>  # delete a core fact

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
[memory]
backend = "sqlite"
path = "~/.arc/memory"
enable_long_term = true
enable_episodic = true

docs.title: Scheduler
docs.text: Schedule recurring or one-time tasks for Arc to run proactively.

docs.title: Creating Jobs
docs.text:
Via chat: 

"Remind me every morning at 9am to check my email"
"Fetch AI news every weekday at 8am"
"In 2 hours, search for the latest NVIDIA stock price"

The main agent creates scheduled jobs using the schedule_job tool.

docs.title: Trigger Types
docs.text: Type Description Example cron Standard 5-field cron expression 0 9 * * 1-5 (weekdays at 9am) interval Every N seconds 1800 (every 30 minutes) oneshot Fire once at a specific time fire_after_seconds=3600 (in 1 hour)

docs.title: With or Without Tools
docs.text:
Jobs can run in two modes:

  • Without tools (default) — plain LLM text generation. Good for reminders, tips, summaries.
  • With tools — full sub-agent with tool access. Good for tasks that need live data (web search, file reading).

docs.title: Managing Jobs
docs.text:
# CLI
arc task list                    # see scheduled jobs

# In chat
/jobs              # list all jobs
/jobs cancel <name>  # cancel a job

Or use the Scheduler tab in the dashboard.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
When a job fires, results are delivered through the NotificationRouter:

  1. Telegram (if configured)
  2. CLI (if arc chat is open)
  3. File log (~/.arc/notifications.log, always)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
[scheduler]
enabled = true
db_path = "~/.arc/scheduler.db"
poll_interval = 30  # seconds between checks

docs.title: Skills And MCP Docs Moved
docs.text:
This topic has been split into separate pages so it is easier to understand.

Use these pages instead:

  • Capabilities Overview
  • Skills
  • MCP
  • Build Your Own Skill

docs.title: Voice Input
docs.text:
Say a wake word and Arc listens. No terminal or browser tab needs to be focused.

You:  "Hey Jarvis"
🔔    *chime*
You:  "Search for flights from Delhi to Mumbai next week"
Arc:  *runs the query, sends you a desktop notification with results*
You:  "Sort by cheapest"    ← no wake word needed, conversation is still active
Arc:  *responds*
      ... 30 seconds of silence ...
Arc:  *goes back to sleep*

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Wake word — openwakeword detects "Hey Jarvis" (configurable) at ~2% CPU idle
  • Speech-to-text — faster-whisper (offline, local, ~150MB model) transcribes after wake word
  • Gateway client — sends transcribed text to arc gateway via WebSocket
  • Conversation flow — 4-state machine: SLEEPING → ACTIVE → PROCESSING → LISTENING (30s follow-up)
  • Cross-platform — conversations appear live in WebChat and Telegram
  • Mic goes deaf during processing — your meetings and background chatter are never captured

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
pip install arc-agent[voice]

# Terminal 1:
arc gateway

# Terminal 2:
arc listen

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
[voice]
wake_model = "hey_jarvis"     # also: alexa, hey_mycroft, hey_rhasspy
whisper_model = "base.en"     # tiny.en (faster) or small.en (more accurate)
silence_duration = 1.5        # seconds of silence = end of speech
listen_timeout = 30.0         # seconds before going back to sleep

docs.title: YAML Workflows
docs.text:
Define repeatable multi-step tasks in YAML. Unlike ad-hoc tool calls, workflows execute in a fixed order with retry, failure handling, human-in-the-loop pauses, and progress events.

Drop .yaml files in ~/.arc/workflows/ — discovered automatically.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# ~/.arc/workflows/jira-rca.yaml
name: jira-rca
description: Root cause analysis for a Jira ticket
trigger: "rca|root cause"
steps:
  - do: Fetch the Jira ticket details
    tool: mcp_call
    args: {server: jira, tool: get_issue, arguments: {key: "${ticket}"}}
  - do: Search the codebase for the relevant error
    retry: 2
  - do: Analyze the root cause and write a summary
    on_fail: continue
  - do: Post the RCA summary as a comment on the ticket

docs.title: Step Formats
docs.text:
# Simple — plain English
steps:
  - search the web for NVIDIA news
  - summarize the results

# Extended — with control options
steps:
  - do: Search for relevant data
    retry: 2
    on_fail: continue        # continue|stop (default: stop)
    ask_if_unclear: true     # agent asks user instead of guessing
    wait_for_input: true     # pause and wait for user response

# Explicit tool call — bypass agent
steps:
  - do: Get the Jira ticket
    tool: mcp_call
    args: {server: jira, tool: get_issue, arguments: {key: "PROJ-123"}}

# Shell command
steps:
  - do: Check running pods
    shell: kubectl get pods -n payments

docs.title: Human-in-the-Loop
docs.text:
Workflows pause and wait for user input in two ways:

Explicit — mark a step with wait_for_input: true

Implicit — if the agent's response is a question, the workflow automatically pauses

In both cases, the workflow waits indefinitely. Your answer is injected as context for the next step. Works across CLI, WebChat, and voice.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
/workflow list              # see available workflows
/workflow jira-rca          # run a workflow

Or via chat: "run the jira-rca workflow for PROJ-1234"

docs.title: Multi-Step Chains and Reviews
docs.text: This is where Arc moves from simple task queueing to structured multi-agent work.

docs.title: What A Chain Is
docs.text:
A chain is one task with multiple ordered steps.

Example:

  • step 1: researcher
  • step 2: writer
  • step 3: reviewer or human review

docs.title: Current Model In Arc
docs.text:
A step currently stores:

  • which agent runs the step
  • which reviewer checks it, if any

The chain as a whole stores the main task instruction.

So today, the recommended pattern is:

  • write one clear overall task description
  • let each step's agent role determine how it contributes
  • rely on previous task activity to carry the output forward

docs.title: Dashboard Support
docs.text:
The dashboard task form supports building multi-step chains.

You can:

  • start with the main task description
  • add steps with the Add Step control
  • choose an agent per step
  • choose a reviewer per step

That is the easiest UI for users who do not want to write chains only in chat or CLI.

docs.title: Example Chain
docs.text:
A research-to-writing flow might look like:

  • step 1: researcher
  • step 2: writer reviewed by human

The reviewer can also be another agent.

docs.title: How Handoff Works
docs.text:
When Arc advances to the next step, the next agent sees:

  • the original task title and instruction
  • a message that previous steps are complete
  • previous activity on the task
  • review feedback if the step is a revision pass

This is designed so the next agent continues the work instead of restarting it.

docs.title: Review Flow
docs.text:
If a step has review_by configured, the reviewer checks the output.

Possible outcomes:

  • approved: move forward
  • needs revision: bounce back to the same agent that produced the work

That bounce stays within the current review stage until approved or the bounce limit is reached.

docs.title: Bounce Limit Behavior
docs.text:
max_bounces applies to a review stage, not effectively to the whole chain.

Arc resets the counter when the task advances to the next step.

docs.title: Separate Tasks vs One Chain
docs.text:
Use separate tasks linked by --after when:

  • each unit should stand on its own
  • you want separate task IDs and history
  • the work is better modeled as multiple queue items

Use one multi-step chain when:

  • you want one task to flow through several agents
  • you want a single shared task history
  • you want reviews inside the same task

docs.title: Create Agents
docs.text:
Task agents are named background workers.

Each agent can have a different role, prompt, and model setup.

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
Open the dashboard and go to the Agents tab.

The agent form supports:

  • name
  • role
  • personality
  • full system prompt
  • provider
  • model
  • base URL
  • API key
  • max concurrency

That means users do not need to edit config files just to give a task agent its own endpoint or API key.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
arc agent create researcher \
  --role "Deep web research" \
  --personality "thorough, cites sources" \
  --model openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514

The CLI is convenient for quick creation, but the dashboard gives fuller visibility for agent setup.

docs.title: Agent File
docs.text:
Agents are stored in:

  • ~/.arc/agents/<name>.toml

Example:

name = "researcher"
role = "AI industry analyst"
personality = "thorough, evidence-driven"
max_concurrent = 1

[llm]
provider = "codex"
model = "codex-mini-latest"
base_url = "https://example.internal/v1"
api_key = "sk-secret"

system_prompt = """
You are a research agent.
Always cite sources and separate findings from assumptions.
"""

docs.title: How Provider Inheritance Works
docs.text:
• If an agent has its own [llm] block, it uses that.
  • If it does not, it falls back to the global default provider from ~/.arc/config.toml.

docs.title: When To Give An Agent Its Own Full Prompt
docs.text:
Use system_prompt when you want tight control.

Use role and personality when a lighter setup is enough.

If system_prompt is present, it overrides the auto-generated prompt for that agent.

docs.title: Multi-Agent Overview
docs.text:
Arc's multi-agent system is for background work handled by named task agents.

You do not need this to use Arc, but it becomes valuable when one chat agent is no longer enough.

docs.title: What Multi-Agent Means In Arc
docs.text:
It means all of the following together:

  • named task agents
  • a persistent task queue
  • optional chains with multiple steps
  • optional AI or human review
  • the gateway processing work in the background

docs.title: When To Use It
docs.text:
Use multi-agent when you want:

  • specialized workers like researcher, writer, or reviewer
  • queued jobs that survive restarts
  • work to continue while you are away
  • different models for different roles
  • structured chains and review loops

docs.title: Task agents
docs.text: These are named workers saved in ~/.arc/agents/*.toml.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text: These are queued pieces of work stored persistently.

docs.title: Chains
docs.text: One task can pass through multiple agents in order.

docs.title: Reviews
docs.text: A step can be reviewed by another agent or by a human.

docs.title: Gateway
docs.text: arc gateway runs the dashboard and the background processor that actually picks up queued tasks.

docs.title: The Basic Flow
docs.text:
1. create one or more task agents
  2. queue a task or chain
  3. run arc gateway
  4. Arc processes the work and stores comments, results, and review state

docs.title: Where To Go Next
docs.text:
• Create Agents
  • Queue Tasks
  • Multi-Step Chains and Reviews

docs.title: Queue Tasks
docs.text:
Tasks are the units of background work that task agents pick up.

They are stored persistently, so they survive restarts.

docs.title: Simple Task
docs.text: arc task add "Find top AI startups funded this year" --assign researcher

docs.title: With Priority
docs.text:
arc task add "Urgent: check incident summary" --assign researcher --priority 1

Lower numbers mean higher priority.

docs.title: Dependent Task
docs.text:
arc task add "Research AI frameworks" --assign researcher
arc task add "Compare the top 3" --assign researcher --after t-001

--after means this task waits for another task to finish.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text: Good for quick scripting and explicit control.

docs.title: Chat
docs.text: You can ask the main agent to queue tasks for named task agents.

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: The Task Board supports creating tasks directly from the UI.

docs.title: Task Statuses
docs.text:
Common statuses:

  • queued
  • in_progress
  • in_review
  • awaiting_human
  • blocked
  • done
  • failed
  • cancelled

docs.title: Replying To A Task
docs.text:
When a task is blocked or waiting for review:

arc task reply t-12345678 "Looks good" --action approve
arc task reply t-12345678 "Please make it more concise" --action revise

docs.title: Requirement To Actually Process Tasks
docs.text:
Queued tasks only get picked up when the background processor is running.

That means you need:

arc gateway

If arc gateway is not running, tasks can stay in queued state.

docs.title: Providers Overview
docs.text:
Providers are how Arc talks to models.

Arc supports both local and remote setups.

docs.title: Global Default Provider
docs.text:
The provider you choose in arc init becomes the default provider for the main agent and for any task agent that does not define its own override.

This global config lives in:

  • ~/.arc/config.toml

Common fields are:

  • default_provider
  • default_model
  • base_url
  • api_key

docs.title: Supported Provider Styles
docs.text:
Arc currently supports presets for:

  • Ollama
  • OpenAI
  • OpenRouter
  • Groq
  • Together
  • LM Studio
  • Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoints
  • Codex / Responses-style endpoints

Some providers come with a built-in default base URL. Others, such as custom endpoints and many Codex/Responses-style setups, need you to provide the base URL explicitly.

docs.title: A Good Mental Model
docs.text:
• Use the global provider for the main agent and as the default fallback.
  • Override the provider per task agent when different workers need different models or endpoints.

docs.title: Simple local setup
docs.text:
• global provider: Ollama
  • main agent uses local model
  • task agents also inherit local model unless overridden

docs.title: Mixed setup
docs.text:
• global provider: Ollama or OpenAI
  • researcher uses a cheap fast model
  • writer uses a stronger writing model
  • reviewer uses a strict model or a different provider

docs.title: Internal endpoint setup
docs.text:
• global or per-agent provider points to a custom base URL
  • API key stored globally or per agent as needed

docs.title: Where To Configure Things
docs.text:
• arc init: main agent and default provider
  • dashboard agent form: per-agent provider/model/base URL/API key
  • ~/.arc/config.toml: global config
  • ~/.arc/agents/*.toml: task agent overrides

docs.title: Per-Agent Provider Config
docs.text: Per-agent provider config is what lets Arc run different task agents on different models.

docs.title: What You Can Override Per Agent
docs.text:
A task agent can override:

  • provider
  • model
  • base URL
  • API key

If those are not set, the agent uses the global defaults.

docs.title: Dashboard Support
docs.text:
The dashboard agent creation form already supports the full provider block.

Users can set:

  • LLM provider
  • model
  • base URL
  • API key

So for task agents, editing files is optional, not required.

docs.title: Example Agent Config
docs.text:
name = "writer"
role = "Turn research into polished writing"

[llm]
provider = "openai"
model = "gpt-4o"
base_url = "https://api.openai.com/v1"
api_key = "sk-..."

docs.title: Example: Internal Codex-Compatible Endpoint
docs.text:
name = "coder"
role = "Implementation agent"

[llm]
provider = "codex"
model = "codex-mini-latest"
base_url = "https://internal.example.com/20250206/app/litellm"
api_key = "sk-..."

docs.title: When To Override
docs.text:
Override per agent when:

  • one role needs a different model quality level
  • one role should use a cheaper or faster endpoint
  • one role must point at an internal gateway
  • one role needs different credentials

docs.title: When Not To Override
docs.text: Do not override unless you need to. Inheriting the global provider keeps setup simpler.

docs.title: Custom Main-Agent Personality Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (if subagents available) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Add a custom personality option in arc init that accepts a full system prompt for the main agent only.

Architecture: Extend setup flow to collect a full prompt when custom is selected, persist it in identity.md, and update SoulManager parsing to treat personality: custom as prompt sourced from ## How I Behave. Keep named task-agent behavior unchanged.

Tech Stack: Python, Typer/Rich setup wizard, pytest.

docs.title: Task 1: Add failing tests for custom prompt persistence and loading
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: tests/identity/test_soul.py - Modify: tests/cli/test_commands.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Write failing tests
  • [ ] Step 2: Run targeted tests and confirm failure
  • [ ] Step 3: Implement minimal runtime + setup changes
  • [ ] Step 4: Re-run targeted tests and confirm pass
  • [ ] Step 5: Run broader related tests

docs.title: Task 2: Implement setup + soul changes
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: arc/identity/setup.py - Modify: arc/identity/soul.py - Optionally modify: arc/identity/personality.py (if helper entries needed)

  • [ ] Step 1: Add Custom personality option in init menus
  • [ ] Step 2: Prompt for full custom system prompt
  • [ ] Step 3: Persist custom prompt in identity.md
  • [ ] Step 4: Load custom prompt from identity.md for main agent system prompt
  • [ ] Step 5: Keep preset path unchanged and keep named task agents untouched

docs.title: Dashboard Agent Provider Config Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (if subagents available) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Let dashboard-created agents store full per-agent LLM configuration, including provider, model, base URL, and API key.

Architecture: Extend the dashboard agent form and POST payload to include llm_base_url and llm_api_key, then persist those fields in the server-side AgentDef save path. Keep the fields optional so existing agents and default/global LLM behavior remain unchanged.

Tech Stack: Python, aiohttp, Alpine.js, pytest.

docs.title: Chunk 1: Agent Config UI and Persistence
Task 1: Add failing tests for API persistence
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: tests/gateway/test_gateway.py - Modify: tests/tasks if a more direct persistence test is needed

  • [ ] Step 1: Write the failing test
  • [ ] Step 2: Run the targeted test and confirm it fails
  • [ ] Step 3: Implement the minimal backend changes
  • [ ] Step 4: Re-run the targeted test and confirm it passes

docs.title: Task 2: Add dashboard fields and payload wiring
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: arc/gateway/templates/dashboard.html - Modify: arc/gateway/server.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Add base_url and api_key fields to the create-agent form
  • [ ] Step 2: Include those values in the create-agent request payload
  • [ ] Step 3: Persist them through AgentDef creation
  • [ ] Step 4: Keep existing agent creation and default LLM behavior unchanged

docs.title: Task 3: Run related verification
docs.text:
Files: - Verify: tests/gateway/test_gateway.py - Verify: tests/identity/test_setup.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Run targeted gateway tests
  • [ ] Step 2: Run related init/setup tests to catch accidental regressions

docs.title: Dashboard Multistep Task Builder Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (if subagents available) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Let users create chained multi-agent tasks from the dashboard, including optional reviewers per step.

Architecture: Extend the dashboard task modal to support a repeatable ordered steps list, keep a shared task instruction, and submit either assigned_agent for simple tasks or steps for chained tasks. Reuse the existing task API, which already supports steps.

Tech Stack: Alpine.js, aiohttp, pytest.

docs.title: Task 1: Add failing regression for task builder controls
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: tests/gateway/test_gateway.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Write the failing test
  • [ ] Step 2: Run the targeted test and confirm it fails
  • [ ] Step 3: Implement the minimal dashboard changes
  • [ ] Step 4: Re-run the targeted test and confirm it passes

docs.title: Task 2: Wire multistep modal state to existing task API
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: arc/gateway/templates/dashboard.html

  • [ ] Step 1: Add step list UI with agent and reviewer
  • [ ] Step 2: Add add/remove/reorder controls
  • [ ] Step 3: Submit steps when present, otherwise submit assigned_agent
  • [ ] Step 4: Reset modal state cleanly after create

docs.title: Task Step Handoff Prompt Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (if subagents available) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Strengthen multi-step task prompts so each agent clearly understands prior steps are complete and it should only perform its current role-specific work.

Architecture: Update task prompt assembly in the task processor to add explicit step/handoff guidance for multi-step tasks. Verify with focused tests against _build_prompt.

Tech Stack: Python, pytest.

docs.title: Task 1: Add failing prompt test
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: tests/tasks/test_processor.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Write the failing test
  • [ ] Step 2: Run the targeted test and confirm it fails
  • [ ] Step 3: Implement the minimal prompt change
  • [ ] Step 4: Re-run the targeted test and confirm it passes

docs.title: Task 2: Verify task regression slice
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: arc/tasks/processor.py - Verify: tests/tasks/test_processor.py - Verify: tests/tasks/test_skill.py - Verify: tests/tasks/test_store.py

  • [ ] Step 1: Add explicit multi-step handoff wording
  • [ ] Step 2: Preserve existing single-step behavior
  • [ ] Step 3: Run related task tests

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
Make Arc's documentation easier to understand, faster to get started with, and clearer about how the system is configured and used as a whole.

The redesign should:

  • lead with the fastest path to a working setup
  • clearly explain the difference between the main agent and task agents
  • give multi-agent/task-agent concepts one dedicated home instead of scattering them
  • explain features in plain language before technical detail
  • make skills, MCP, and custom extension paths easy to discover

docs.title: 1. The onboarding path is too heavy
docs.text: The current quickstart and homepage introduce named agents and task queueing too early. That makes the system feel more complex than it needs to be for first-time users.

docs.title: 2. Task-agent concepts are scattered
docs.text: Information about agents, tasks, reviews, and multi-step work is spread across README.md, docs/index.md, docs/getting-started.md, docs/cli.md, and the docs/taskforce/* pages. This makes it harder to form a clear mental model.

docs.title: 3. Feature pages assume too much context
docs.text: Several pages describe what a feature does technically, but do not start with what the feature is for, when a user should use it, and how it fits into the overall product.

docs.title: 4. Skills/customization are not discoverable enough
docs.text:
Users need a clearer path for understanding:

  • built-in capabilities
  • local custom skills
  • MCP-based extensions
  • how Arc can be extended without editing core code

docs.title: Fastest path first
docs.text:
The first thing a new user should see is the shortest path to:

  1. install Arc
  2. run arc init
  3. talk to the main agent with arc chat
  4. run arc gateway when they want the dashboard/background systems

docs.title: Concept before configuration
docs.text:
Each section should first explain:

  • what this is
  • when you use it
  • why it exists

Only then should it explain commands, flags, and file layout.

docs.title: One home per mental model
docs.text:
Each major concept should have one obvious place where users learn it:

  • main interactive assistant: Core Concepts / Getting Started
  • task agents and chains: Multi-Agent
  • browser, memory, scheduler, skills, MCP: Capabilities
  • model providers and endpoint configuration: Providers

docs.title: Reduce jargon at the top level
docs.text: The docs should avoid opening with internal terms like "taskforce" unless those terms are introduced naturally and clearly.

docs.title: Proposed Navigation
docs.text: Use top horizontal tabs in MkDocs Material for primary product areas.

docs.title: Top Navigation
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • Core Concepts
  • Capabilities
  • Multi-Agent
  • Providers
  • Reference

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Purpose: help a new user get Arc running fast without learning the whole system first.

Suggested pages:

  • Fastest Setup
  • First Chat
  • Run the Gateway
  • Common First Tasks

This section should not require users to create task agents immediately.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Purpose: explain how Arc works in easy terms.

Suggested pages:

  • What Arc Is
  • Main Agent vs Task Agents
  • Tasks vs Multi-Step Tasks
  • Reviews and Human Input

This is where the docs should clearly explain:

  • the main agent is the assistant you talk to directly
  • task agents are named background workers for queued jobs

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
Purpose: explain the built-in product features and how users extend Arc.

Suggested pages:

  • Built-in Capabilities
  • Browser
  • Dashboard
  • Memory
  • Scheduler
  • Skills
  • MCP
  • Build Your Own Skill

Each page should begin with:

  • what this capability is for
  • when users would use it
  • how to enable/configure it

docs.title: Multi-Agent
docs.text:
Purpose: give task-agent and multi-agent behavior one dedicated home.

Suggested pages:

  • Overview
  • Create Agents
  • Queue Tasks
  • Multi-Step Chains
  • Reviews and Bounce Limits

This section should become the single source of truth for:

  • named task agents
  • queued background work
  • chain execution
  • human review
  • agent reviewers
  • bounce limits

docs.title: Providers
docs.text:
Purpose: clearly explain LLM/provider configuration at both system and per-agent level.

Suggested pages:

  • Provider Overview
  • Local Providers
  • Cloud Providers
  • Custom Endpoints
  • Per-Agent Provider Config

This section should explain:

  • global/default provider config
  • main agent provider usage
  • per-agent provider overrides
  • base URL / API key expectations

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
Purpose: keep reference material discoverable but separate from the learning path.

Suggested pages:

  • CLI
  • Configuration
  • Architecture

docs.title: Existing Files To Rewrite
docs.text:
• README.md
  • docs/index.md
  • docs/getting-started.md
  • docs/cli.md
  • docs/configuration.md
  • docs/taskforce/overview.md
  • docs/taskforce/agents.md
  • docs/taskforce/tasks.md

docs.title: Existing Files To Keep But Reposition
docs.text:
• docs/features/browser.md
  • docs/features/memory.md
  • docs/features/dashboard.md
  • docs/features/scheduler.md
  • docs/features/skills-mcp.md
  • docs/architecture.md

docs.title: New Pages To Add
docs.text:
• docs/core/what-is-arc.md
  • docs/core/main-agent-vs-task-agents.md
  • docs/core/tasks-vs-multi-step-tasks.md
  • docs/core/reviews-and-human-input.md
  • docs/capabilities/built-in-capabilities.md
  • docs/capabilities/build-your-own-skill.md
  • docs/providers/overview.md
  • docs/providers/per-agent-config.md
  • docs/multi-agent/multi-step-chains.md
  • docs/multi-agent/reviews-and-bounces.md

docs.title: Homepage
docs.text:
The homepage should:

  • describe Arc in one simple paragraph
  • show the shortest possible setup path
  • list the main product areas
  • link users to the right next page depending on intent

It should not front-load agent creation or task queue commands.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
The getting started page should focus on:

  • installation
  • arc init
  • arc chat
  • arc gateway

Then it can mention optional next steps like task agents and dashboard features.

docs.title: Multi-Agent Docs
docs.text:
The multi-agent section should explain in plain language:

  • what a task agent is
  • when to create one
  • how task chains work
  • how reviews work
  • what the current UI surfaces can and cannot do

docs.title: Skills Docs
docs.text:
The capabilities section should include a dedicated skill page that explains:

  • what built-in skills are
  • what custom skills are
  • where local custom skills live
  • how MCP relates to skills
  • when a user should choose custom skills vs MCP

docs.title: Navigation Behavior
docs.text:
MkDocs Material should be configured so:

  • top navigation presents the primary sections as horizontal tabs
  • left navigation presents sub-pages for the selected section
  • the docs feel segmented by topic without losing discoverability

docs.title: Keep terminology stable once introduced
docs.text: The docs may still mention "task agents" and "multi-agent", but those terms should be introduced with plain-English definitions before using them repeatedly.

docs.title: Reuse content where it already works
docs.text: The goal is not to rewrite every page from scratch. Strong existing feature explanations can be kept, trimmed, and moved into the new structure.

docs.title: Prioritize comprehension over completeness on entry pages
docs.text: Users should be able to understand the system shape before they encounter all advanced options.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
The documentation redesign is successful if:

  • a new user can get Arc running without reading about agents first
  • the difference between main agent and task agents is obvious
  • task-agent concepts are not scattered across unrelated pages
  • users can find how to add custom skills without hunting through features pages
  • the information architecture feels product-shaped rather than implementation-shaped

docs.title: Agent Docs Moved
docs.text:
This content now lives under Multi-Agent and Providers.

Start here:

  • Create Agents
  • Per-Agent Provider Config
  • Main Agent vs Task Agents

docs.title: Multi-Agent Docs Moved
docs.text:
The old Taskforce section has been replaced by the clearer Multi-Agent section.

Use these pages instead:

  • Multi-Agent Overview
  • Create Agents
  • Queue Tasks
  • Multi-Step Chains and Reviews

docs.title: Task Queue Docs Moved
docs.text:
This content now lives under Multi-Agent.

Use these pages instead:

  • Queue Tasks
  • Multi-Step Chains and Reviews
  • Tasks, Chains, and Reviews

docs.title: Workflow Chain Docs Moved
docs.text:
The task-agent chain documentation now lives here:

  • Multi-Step Chains and Reviews
  • Tasks, Chains, and Reviews

If you are looking for YAML workflows, use Workflows (YAML).
